glossary.definition: A dial that controls how creative the AI is; a low number makes it predictable and factual, while a high number makes it wild and varied.
A filter that chops off the least likely words before the AI even considers them, keeping its vocabulary grounded.
What happens when you set the temperature to 0, forcing the AI to always pick the single most mathematically probable next word.

hook.scenario: You set temperature=0 for a customer support bot to get consistent answers. A colleague says 'great, it's fully deterministic now.' Then two identical queries return slightly different phrasing.
hook.question: Is temperature 0 really deterministic — and if not, what actually controls how 'random' the model is?

mental_model.intuition: Temperature is the dial between 'always pick the most likely word' and 'pick any word at all'. Top-p is the filter that limits which words are even in the lottery.
mental_model.description: At every token step, the model produces a probability distribution over its entire vocabulary — 'what should come next?' Temperature reshapes that distribution: low temperature makes the highest-probability word overwhelmingly dominant; high temperature flattens the distribution so less-likely words get real chances. Top-p (nucleus sampling) cuts the vocabulary before sampling — keeping only enough top words to cover probability mass p, discarding the long tail of unlikely tokens.

sections.body: Picture the model at each step choosing the next token from a sorted bar chart — probabilities on the y-axis, candidate tokens on the x-axis. With no intervention, it would always pick the tallest bar (argmax). That would be repetitive and robotic. Temperature changes the bar heights before the pick.

image.alt: Two distributions: low temperature is peaky on one token; high temperature is flat across all.

sections.recap: Temperature reshapes the probability distribution — low = peaked, high = flat.

sections.body: Mathematically, each logit (raw model score) is divided by the temperature before the softmax. Temperature=1 leaves the distribution unchanged. Temperature < 1 (e.g. 0.2) sharpens it: the top token gains probability mass, others lose it. Temperature > 1 flattens it: even low-probability tokens become competitive. You don't need the math — just hold the picture: colder = more predictable, hotter = more creative.
sections.recap: Lower T → model concentrates on the most-likely token. Higher T → spreads probability, more variety.

sections.body: Top-p (nucleus sampling) is the companion filter. Before sampling, sort tokens by probability highest-to-lowest and keep adding until the cumulative mass reaches p. Discard everything outside that nucleus. top_p=0.9 means 'sample only from the smallest set of tokens that together account for 90% of the probability.' Low-probability tokens in the long tail are never picked, even if temperature is high.

image.alt: A sorted probability bar chart with a top-p cutoff keeping the top bars summing to 0.9.

sections.recap: Top-p cuts the vocabulary to the 'nucleus' — the smallest set summing to probability p.

sections.body: In practice: use temperature for tuning creativity vs precision, and leave top_p at 1 (off). Or use top_p as the primary control and leave temperature at 1. OpenAI recommends not adjusting both at once — they interact in confusing ways. For factual tasks (extraction, classification): temp 0–0.2. For creative writing or ideation: temp 0.7–1.0.
sections.recap: Tune one: temperature for precision/creativity; top_p for vocabulary ceiling. Not both at once.

sections.body: The gotcha: temperature=0 is NOT fully deterministic on the OpenAI API. At this setting the model uses greedy decoding (picks the argmax token) but floating-point arithmetic on distributed GPUs can produce tiny rounding differences that occasionally flip two equally-likely tokens. True reproducibility also requires setting the seed parameter. Even then, OpenAI does not guarantee identical outputs across model versions.
sections.recap: Temperature=0 ≈ deterministic, not truly deterministic. Use seed + same model version for best repeatability.

code_snippets.title: Set temperature for a factual extraction task
code_snippets.code:
from openai import OpenAI
client = OpenAI()

response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model='gpt-4o',
    messages=[
        {'role': 'system', 'content': 'Extract the invoice total. Reply with just the number.'},
        {'role': 'user',   'content': 'Invoice #4821: subtotal $120, tax $10.80, total $130.80.'},
    ],
    temperature=0.0,   # factual — want the most likely token, not creativity
    seed=42,           # improves (but does not guarantee) reproducibility
)
print(response.choices[0].message.content)  # '130.80'
code_snippets.explanation: For extraction and classification tasks, use temperature=0 to maximize consistency. Add seed to improve reproducibility — but test on your model version; a model update may still change outputs.

code_snippets.title: Higher temperature for creative brainstorming
code_snippets.code:
from openai import OpenAI
client = OpenAI()

response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model='gpt-4o',
    messages=[
        {'role': 'system', 'content': 'You are a creative product-naming assistant.'},
        {'role': 'user',   'content': 'Suggest 5 names for a meal-planning app targeting gym-goers.'},
    ],
    temperature=0.9,   # more variety — different names each run
    top_p=1.0,         # top_p off (=1 means 'keep everything')
)
print(response.choices[0].message.content)
code_snippets.explanation: For ideation tasks, a higher temperature generates more diverse options across runs. Leaving top_p=1 keeps it out of the picture so you're only tweaking one dial.

common_mistakes.title: Believing temperature=0 is fully deterministic
common_mistakes.explanation: Floating-point non-determinism on GPU clusters and model updates mean temperature=0 only maximises consistency — it does not guarantee identical outputs. If repeatability matters critically, also set seed and pin the model version.

common_mistakes.title: Adjusting both temperature and top_p simultaneously
common_mistakes.explanation: They interact: a high temperature widens the distribution, then top_p cuts it; a low temperature narrows it so top_p cuts almost nothing. Tweak one, leave the other at its neutral value (temperature=1 or top_p=1), so you know what you changed.

recall_questions.q: What does temperature actually change, mechanically?
recall_questions.answer: It divides each logit by the temperature value before softmax, reshaping the probability distribution. Low values sharpen it (one token dominates); high values flatten it (more tokens are competitive).

recall_questions.q: What does top_p=0.9 mean in plain terms?
recall_questions.answer: Keep only the smallest set of top candidate tokens whose probabilities sum to 90%, and sample exclusively from that set. Tokens in the long tail are discarded.

recall_questions.q: Is temperature=0 fully deterministic on the OpenAI API?
recall_questions.answer: No — floating-point non-determinism across distributed GPUs can still produce slight variation. Use temperature=0 plus the seed parameter for best reproducibility, and accept that model updates may still change outputs.

oa_questions.question: You're deploying a medical info bot. A colleague sets temperature=1.5 to 'make it friendlier'. What problems does this introduce?
oa_questions.answer: High temperature flattens the probability distribution so less-likely (and potentially inaccurate or confabulated) tokens become competitive. In a medical context this dramatically increases the risk of hallucination — the model may confidently output incorrect dosages or conditions. For factual, high-stakes tasks, keep temperature at 0–0.2.
oa_questions.approach: Explain the distribution-flattening effect and tie it directly to hallucination risk. 'Friendlier tone' is a system-prompt problem, not a temperature problem.

oa_questions.question: A developer runs the same prompt 100 times at temperature=0 and gets the exact same output 98 times, but 2 different outputs on 2 runs. They conclude the API is broken. What is the real explanation?
oa_questions.answer: Temperature=0 uses greedy decoding but floating-point arithmetic on distributed GPU clusters introduces tiny rounding differences that can occasionally flip two nearly-equal logits. This is expected behavior, not a bug. To maximise consistency, also set the seed parameter — though even then, OpenAI does not guarantee identical outputs across all conditions.
oa_questions.approach: Name floating-point non-determinism on distributed hardware as the root cause. Distinguish greedy decoding (what temp=0 does) from true determinism (not what the API guarantees).
